title: Speed up `int.__round__` for large negative `ndigits`
author: devdanzin
body:
# Feature or enhancement

### Proposal:

Rounding an int by a large positive ndigits is instantaneous, while a by a large negative number takes ages, only to return zero:

```python
>>> large_number = 2**31
>>> large_negative_number = -2**31
>>> d = 17000
>>> d.__round__(large_number)  # Instant result, returns d
17000
>>> d.__round__(large_negative_number)  # Takes forever, calculating divmod(d, int(pow(10, large_negative_number)))
```

It could be almost as fast as the positive ndigits case by checking:
```python
int(math.log10(d)) < abs(large_negative_number)
```
And returning zero if it's True. 

A similar optimization is possible for `Fraction`: #132472

I realize there may not be a real use case for these enhancements.

cc @JelleZijlstra 

### Has this already been discussed elsewhere?

This is a minor feature, which does not need previous discussion elsewhere

### Links to previous discussion of this feature:

It was very briefly discussed on Discord.

<!-- gh-linked-prs -->
### Linked PRs
* gh-132579
<!-- /gh-linked-prs -->

comments.author: skirpichev
comments.body:
Yes, that does make sense for me, the gmpy2 has similar optimization:
https://github.com/aleaxit/gmpy/blob/ea6cdf16064e8f5bd575c0c1b02df0ea0fa70fc4/src/gmpy2_mpz_misc.c#L269-L272
Though, this must be benchmarked.

But you should be slightly more careful.  It's easy to find a counterexample for your inequality:
```pycon
>>> import math
>>> d = 501
>>> n = -3
>>> round(d, n)
1000
>>> int(math.log10(d)) < abs(n)
True
```

> calculating divmod(d, int(pow(10, large_negative_number)))

It's actually divmod_near().  You can see it's pure-Python equivalent in commentary.

comments.author: devdanzin
comments.body:
> But you should be slightly more careful. It's easy to find a counterexample for your inequality:

Unfortunately that's as far as my math can take me, I have no idea about how to start fixing this (other than brute forcing values to see where errors happen, trying to figure out a pattern and a convoluted way to avoid them).

comments.author: skirpichev
picnixz
comments.body: Can you tests when `round(d, n)` does not give 0 instead? namely, what happens when `d` is very large (possibly much larger than $10^n$)? having benchmarks there would be interesting as well.

comments.author: skirpichev
comments.body:
> Can you tests when round(d, n) does not give 0 instead?

In fact, above tests have such case (n > -5).

> namely, what happens when ``d`` is very large (possibly much larger than ``10**n``)?

divmod() cost will be eventually dominate in running time, I think.

| Benchmark         | ref    | patch                | patch2               | patch3               |
|-------------------|:------:|:--------------------:|:--------------------:|:--------------------:|
| round(2**5, -5)   | 525 ns | 297 ns: 1.77x faster | 532 ns: 1.01x slower | 233 ns: 2.26x faster |
| round(2**7, -5)   | 528 ns | 301 ns: 1.76x faster | 536 ns: 1.01x slower | 234 ns: 2.26x faster |
| round(2**10, -5)  | 554 ns | 298 ns: 1.86x faster | 581 ns: 1.05x slower | 619 ns: 1.12x slower |
| round(2**15, -5)  | 555 ns | 675 ns: 1.22x slower | 586 ns: 1.06x slower | 620 ns: 1.12x slower |
| round(2**20, -5)  | 667 ns | 803 ns: 1.20x slower | 668 ns: 1.00x slower | 730 ns: 1.10x slower |
| round(2**25, -5)  | 751 ns | 877 ns: 1.17x slower | 761 ns: 1.01x slower | 783 ns: 1.04x slower |
| round(2**30, -5)  | 808 ns | 896 ns: 1.11x slower | 784 ns: 1.03x faster | 840 ns: 1.04x slower |
| round(2**50, -5)  | 793 ns | 898 ns: 1.13x slower | 784 ns: 1.01x faster | 830 ns: 1.05x slower |
| round(2**100, -5) | 864 ns | 996 ns: 1.15x slower | not significant      | 908 ns: 1.05x slower |
| Geometric mean    | (ref)  | 1.10x faster         | 1.01x slower         | 1.14x faster         |

(Here ``round(2**d, -5) != 0`` for ``d>=20``.)

<details>

<summary>Benchmark code and patch3.</summary>

```py
# bench.py
import math
import pyperf
runner = pyperf.Runner()
n = -5
for d in [5, 7, 10, 15, 20, 25, 30, 50, 100]:
    x = 1<<d
    s = f"round(2**{d}, {n})"
    runner.bench_func(s, round, x, n)
```

Patch 3:
```diff
diff --git a/Objects/longobject.c b/Objects/longobject.c
index 692312c1ad..856bbf3c0b 100644
--- a/Objects/longobject.c
+++ b/Objects/longobject.c
@@ -6212,6 +6212,21 @@ int___round___impl(PyObject *self, PyObject *o_ndigits)
         return long_long(self);
     }
 
+    int64_t n, e;
+
+    if (PyLong_AsInt64(ndigits, &n) < 0) {
+        Py_DECREF(ndigits);
+        return NULL;
+    }
+    n = -n;
+
+    (void)_PyLong_Frexp((PyLongObject *)self, &e);
+
+    if (e/3 + 2 < n) {
+        Py_DECREF(ndigits);
+        return _PyLong_GetZero();
+    }
+
     /* result = self - divmod_near(self, 10 ** -ndigits)[1] */
     PyObject *temp = (PyObject*)long_neg((PyLongObject*)ndigits);
     Py_SETREF(ndigits, temp);
```

</details>
